topic: A person accidentally sends a text to the wrong recipient
raw_output:
[story 1]

The screen glowed, a harsh white rectangle in the dim light of the late hour. I’d meant to send a quick, mundane reminder to Sarah about the meeting tomorrow. My thumb hovered over the keyboard, a slight tremor running through my hand. Instead, the message flashed across the display: "Did you see the new movie trailer?" The recipient's name was undeniably wrong—it belonged to Mark, a colleague I barely knew. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I reread the text, the words feeling alien and heavy. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I stared at the phone, my eyes wide, searching for an escape route in the familiar layout of my notifications. A wave of sudden, sickening panic washed over me, making my limbs feel strangely heavy, as if moving required immense effort. I quickly typed a retraction, a frantic scramble to undo the mistake, but the damage was done.

[story 2]

A strange, hollow feeling settled over me as I looked at my phone. I had intended to send a casual anecdote to my friend, but the message had landed squarely in the inbox of my boss. The silence in my small apartment amplified the frantic thumping in my chest. I kept glancing from the screen to my reflection in the darkened window, trying to reconcile the calm facade with the internal turmoil churning beneath the surface. My jaw felt tight, my tongue thick. Every muscle in my neck seemed to be straining. *Oh no. Oh God, no.* I started pacing the small space, my steps quick and uneven, unable to settle. The air felt too thin, and the world seemed to tilt slightly on its axis. I wanted to scream, but no sound emerged; only a dry, rasping sound in my throat.

[story 3]

I blinked several times, trying to clear the fuzzy edges of my vision. The notification banner remained stubbornly visible, mocking me. It was a message I hadn't intended to send, addressed to someone entirely unfamiliar. My fingers, clumsy and unresponsive, fumbled for the delete function. Each tap felt clumsy, disconnected from my will. A profound sense of wrongness washed over me, a dizzying sensation that made the floor seem unsteady beneath my feet. I leaned back against the sofa, my head spinning slightly. My thoughts were a tangled mess, rapid-fire questions looping without forming coherent sentences. I kept repeating the sequence: wrong text, wrong person, complete lack of control. The room seemed to shrink around me, emphasizing the sheer, baffling nature of the predicament.
